_metadata.title: QIF Neurotransmitter Systems Database
_metadata.description: Comprehensive neurotransmitter database with synthesis pathways, cofactor dependencies, receptor subtypes, transporters, and degradation enzymes. Mapped to QIF hourglass bands and DSM-5-TR diagnostic categories. For threat modeling purposes — not clinical diagnosis.
primary_sources: Stahl SM. Stahl's Essential Psychopharmacology. 5th ed. Cambridge University Press, 2021
Kandel ER, et al. Principles of Neural Science. 6th ed. McGraw Hill, 2021
NCBI StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing, 2026
Purves D, et al. Neuroscience. 6th ed. Sinauer Associates, 2018
notes: Gene names follow HUGO Gene Nomenclature Committee (HGNC) conventions.
Receptor subtypes include IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology classifications.
DSM mappings are diagnostic category references for threat modeling, not diagnostic claims.
Dependency chains show cofactors required for synthesis — disruption of any cofactor can impair neurotransmitter production.
Confidence: HIGH = primary literature/textbook, MEDIUM = review paper, LOW = inferred.

chemical_classes.monoamine: Neurotransmitters derived from aromatic amino acids (tyrosine, tryptophan, histidine)
chemical_classes.catecholamine: Monoamine subclass: dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine (all from tyrosine)
chemical_classes.amino_acid: Amino acids acting as neurotransmitters (glutamate, GABA, glycine)
chemical_classes.acetylcholine: Quaternary amine — sole member of its class

neurotransmitters.name: Dopamine

synthesis_pathway.notes: Can also come directly from dietary tyrosine
Rate-limiting step of catecholamine synthesis. TH requires iron as prosthetic group.
Also called DOPA decarboxylase

degradation.notes: MAO-B is the predominant form in striatum. MAO-A also degrades DA in some regions.
COMT Val158Met polymorphism affects PFC dopamine levels. Val = fast degradation, Met = slow.
Final common metabolite of dopamine, measurable in CSF and urine

transporter.name: Dopamine Transporter
transporter.notes: DAT density is low in PFC — COMT and NET handle PFC dopamine clearance instead

receptors.function: Motor activation, reward, working memory facilitation
receptors.clinical: Inverted-U dose-response for working memory in PFC

receptors.signaling: Decreases cAMP, opens K+ channels, reduces Ca2+ entry
receptors.function: Motor inhibition, reward modulation, presynaptic autoreceptor
receptors.clinical: Primary target of all antipsychotics. Blockade reduces psychotic symptoms but causes EPS.

receptors.function: Reward, motivation, cognitive flexibility
receptors.clinical: Target of cariprazine (partial agonist) for bipolar depression

receptors.function: Attention, novelty seeking, cognitive modulation
receptors.clinical: DRD4 7-repeat VNTR polymorphism associated with ADHD (research-based association)

receptors.function: Blood pressure regulation, hippocampal plasticity
receptors.clinical: Higher affinity for DA than D1; may contribute to tonic DA signaling

cofactor_dependencies.role: Prosthetic group for TH; rate-limiting for DA synthesis
cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Reduced DA synthesis → fatigue, cognitive impairment, restless legs syndrome
Impaired DA methylation; SAM synthesis requires B12, folate (B9), methionine

dsm_conditions.name: Schizophrenia
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Mesolimbic DA excess (positive symptoms), mesocortical DA deficit (negative symptoms)

dsm_conditions.name: Parkinson's disease
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Nigrostriatal DA neuron degeneration (>80% loss before motor symptoms)

dsm_conditions.name: ADHD
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Reduced DA signaling in PFC and striatum

dsm_conditions.name: Major depression
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Reduced mesolimbic DA (anhedonia component)

dsm_conditions.name: Cocaine use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: DAT blockade → DA accumulation in nucleus accumbens

dsm_conditions.name: Amphetamine use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: DAT reversal → massive DA release

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: HIGH — DBS of STN modulates DA circuits in Parkinson's. Closed-loop DBS adjusts stimulation based on beta oscillations in basal ganglia (DA-dependent).

neurotransmitters.name: Serotonin

synthesis_pathway.notes: Rate-limiting step. TPH2 is brain-specific. Tryptophan competes with other large neutral amino acids (LNAAs) for BBB transport via LAT1.
Same enzyme (AADC/DDC) as dopamine synthesis

degradation.notes: MAO-A is predominant for 5-HT. Low MAOA activity linked to aggression (Brunner syndrome, MAOA-L variant).

transporter.name: Serotonin Transporter
transporter.notes: 5-HTTLPR polymorphism (short/long) affects SERT expression. Short allele = reduced SERT, stress sensitivity.

receptors.function: Anxiolysis, mood regulation; somatodendritic autoreceptor on raphe neurons
receptors.clinical: Target of buspirone (partial agonist) for anxiety. SSRI therapeutic delay partly due to 5-HT1A autoreceptor desensitization.

receptors.function: Presynaptic terminal autoreceptor, vasoconstriction
receptors.clinical: Target of triptans for migraine (vasoconstriction)

receptors.signaling: Activates PLC → IP3 + DAG → Ca2+ release
receptors.function: Sensory gating, perception, cortical excitability
receptors.clinical: Primary target of classic psychedelics (LSD, psilocybin). Atypical antipsychotics (clozapine, olanzapine) are 5-HT2A antagonists.

receptors.function: Appetite regulation, CSF production, DA modulation
receptors.clinical: Lorcaserin (agonist, withdrawn) for obesity. RNA editing of HTR2C affects receptor function.

receptors.function: Fast excitatory transmission, emesis trigger
receptors.clinical: Ondansetron (antagonist) for chemotherapy-induced nausea

receptors.function: Memory facilitation, GI motility
receptors.clinical: Prucalopride (agonist) for constipation

receptors.function: Cognition, memory
receptors.clinical: Investigational target for cognitive enhancement in Alzheimer's

receptors.function: Circadian rhythm regulation, thermoregulation, mood
receptors.clinical: Involved in antidepressant action of some atypicals (lurasidone)

cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Reduced 5-HT synthesis → mood disturbance, sleep disruption
Acute tryptophan depletion → transient mood lowering in vulnerable individuals

cofactor_dependencies.role: BH4 regeneration via DHFR pathway; SAM cycle
cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Impaired BH4 recycling → reduced monoamine synthesis

dsm_conditions.name: Major depression
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Reduced 5-HT signaling (monoamine hypothesis component)

dsm_conditions.name: Generalized anxiety disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: 5-HT1A hypofunction in limbic circuits

dsm_conditions.name: OCD
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Serotonergic dysfunction in CSTC loop; responds to SSRIs

dsm_conditions.name: PTSD
dsm_conditions.mechanism: 5-HT dysregulation in amygdala-PFC circuit

dsm_conditions.name: Eating disorders
dsm_conditions.mechanism: 5-HT2C and 5-HT1A in hypothalamic appetite circuits

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: MEDIUM — Raphe nuclei are deep structures, not directly accessible by non-invasive BCI. However, cortical 5-HT effects (via 5-HT2A on PFC pyramidal neurons) are measurable via EEG changes.

neurotransmitters.name: Norepinephrine

synthesis_pathway.notes: Occurs inside synaptic vesicles (not cytoplasm). DBH is a copper-containing enzyme.

neurotransmitters.precursor_pathway: Full chain: L-Phe → L-Tyr (PAH, BH4+Fe) → L-DOPA (TH, BH4+Fe) → DA (DDC, B6) → NE (DBH, Cu+VitC)

transporter.name: Norepinephrine Transporter
transporter.notes: NET also clears dopamine in PFC (where DAT density is low)

receptors.function: Vasoconstriction, arousal, PFC modulation at high NE
receptors.clinical: Prazosin (antagonist) for PTSD nightmares

receptors.signaling: Decreases cAMP, opens K+ channels, reduces Ca2+ entry
receptors.function: Presynaptic autoreceptor (reduces NE release); postsynaptic in PFC enhances working memory
receptors.clinical: Clonidine, guanfacine (α2A agonists) for ADHD, PTSD, hypertension

receptors.function: Heart rate increase, memory consolidation (amygdala-dependent)
receptors.clinical: Propranolol (antagonist) blocks emotional memory reconsolidation

receptors.function: Bronchodilation, glycogenolysis in astrocytes
receptors.clinical: Salbutamol (agonist) for asthma

cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Menkes disease: copper deficiency → NE synthesis failure → neurodegeneration

dsm_conditions.name: Major depression
dsm_conditions.mechanism: NE hypofunction (fatigue, psychomotor retardation component)

dsm_conditions.name: PTSD
dsm_conditions.mechanism: LC hyperactivation → NE excess → hyperarousal, nightmares

dsm_conditions.name: ADHD
dsm_conditions.mechanism: NE hypofunction in PFC → inattention

dsm_conditions.name: Social anxiety
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Excessive NE-mediated sympathetic activation

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: MEDIUM — LC is deep brainstem, not directly accessible. But LC-NE system modulates cortical arousal detectable by EEG (P300 amplitude, pupil-linked arousal).

neurotransmitters.name: Acetylcholine

synthesis_pathway.notes: Choline from diet (lecithin/phosphatidylcholine) or recycled from ACh breakdown. Acetyl-CoA from mitochondrial metabolism (glucose → pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA). Choline uptake via CHT1 (SLC5A7) is rate-limiting.

degradation.notes: Extremely fast enzyme — hydrolyzes ACh in <1ms. Present at all cholinergic synapses.

transporter.name: High-Affinity Choline Transporter
transporter.mechanism: Na+-dependent choline reuptake (not ACh reuptake — ACh is degraded extracellularly)
transporter.notes: Unlike monoamines, ACh has no reuptake transporter. Instead, AChE degrades it and choline is recycled.

receptors.function: Attention, arousal, cognitive enhancement
receptors.clinical: Most abundant brain nAChR. Target of nicotine. Mutations cause autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE).

receptors.function: Synaptic plasticity, anti-inflammatory (cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway), working memory
receptors.clinical: Reduced in schizophrenia. Target for cognitive enhancement research.

receptors.function: Memory, learning, cortical excitability
receptors.clinical: Key target for Alzheimer's cognitive enhancement. Xanomeline (M1/M4 agonist) in clinical trials.

receptors.function: Presynaptic autoreceptor, cardiac deceleration
receptors.clinical: Vagal tone mediator. Atropine (antagonist) blocks cardiac M2.

receptors.function: Smooth muscle contraction, glandular secretion
receptors.clinical: Anticholinergic side effects of antipsychotics (dry mouth, constipation)

receptors.function: Motor inhibition, DA modulation in striatum
receptors.clinical: M4 PAMs (positive allosteric modulators) as novel antipsychotics (reduce DA without D2 blockade)

receptors.function: Cerebral vasodilation, DA release modulation in VTA
receptors.clinical: Least abundant mAChR. Role in reward/addiction via VTA DA modulation.

cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Impaired ACh synthesis, fatty liver, neural tube defects in pregnancy

cofactor_dependencies.role: Acetyl group donor from mitochondrial metabolism
cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Glucose deprivation → reduced Acetyl-CoA → impaired ACh synthesis (brain vulnerability to hypoglycemia)

cofactor_dependencies.role: Required for pyruvate dehydrogenase → Acetyl-CoA production
cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome: B1 deficiency → reduced ACh → memory impairment

dsm_conditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Basal forebrain cholinergic neuron degeneration (nucleus basalis of Meynert)

dsm_conditions.name: Parkinson's dementia
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Cholinergic loss compounds DA loss → cognitive decline

dsm_conditions.name: Delirium
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Acute cholinergic deficit (anticholinergic drugs are #1 cause)

dsm_conditions.name: Nicotine use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: nAChR (α4β2) desensitization and upregulation

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: MEDIUM — Cholinergic modulation affects EEG oscillations (theta in hippocampus, gamma in cortex). DBS in nucleus basalis of Meynert explored for Alzheimer's.

neurotransmitters.name: Glutamate

synthesis_pathway.notes: Primary synthesis route in neurons. Glutamine is supplied by astrocytes (glutamate-glutamine cycle).
Links excitatory neurotransmission to energy metabolism

degradation.notes: Glutamate-glutamine cycle: neurons release Glu → astrocytes convert to Gln → Gln shuttled back to neurons

transporter.name: Excitatory Amino Acid Transporters
transporter.notes: EAAT2/GLT-1 handles ~90% of glutamate clearance. Astrocytic. Loss of EAAT2 → excitotoxicity.

vesicular_transporter.name: Vesicular Glutamate Transporters
vesicular_transporter.notes: VGLUT1: cortex, hippocampus. VGLUT2: thalamus, brainstem. VGLUT3: serotonergic/cholinergic neurons (co-release).

receptors.gating: Voltage-dependent Mg2+ block at resting potential; requires simultaneous glutamate + glycine/D-serine co-agonist binding AND membrane depolarization
receptors.function: Synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD), memory formation, coincidence detection
receptors.clinical: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis (autoimmune). Ketamine (channel blocker) for treatment-resistant depression. Memantine for Alzheimer's. Phencyclidine (PCP) causes schizophrenia-like psychosis via NMDA blockade.

receptors.function: Fast excitatory transmission (milliseconds). AMPA receptor trafficking is the primary mechanism of LTP expression.
receptors.clinical: Perampanel (antagonist) for epilepsy. AMPAkines investigated for cognitive enhancement.
receptors.notes: GluA2 subunit contains Q/R editing (by ADAR2 enzyme) that makes the channel Ca2+-impermeable. Failure of GluA2 editing → excitotoxicity.

receptors.function: Presynaptic modulation of glutamate and GABA release, hippocampal mossy fiber transmission
receptors.clinical: GluK2 mutations linked to intellectual disability. Kainate injection causes temporal lobe epilepsy model.

receptors.function: Postsynaptic excitation, synaptic plasticity, LTD at cerebellar parallel fiber synapses
receptors.clinical: mGluR5 NAMs investigated for Fragile X syndrome. mGluR5 implicated in addiction (glutamatergic drive).

receptors.function: Presynaptic inhibition of glutamate release (autoreceptor)
receptors.clinical: mGluR2/3 agonists investigated for schizophrenia (reduce excess glutamate release)

receptors.function: Presynaptic inhibition. mGluR6 is unique — ON bipolar cells in retina (inverts glutamate signal for light response).
receptors.clinical: mGluR4 PAMs investigated for Parkinson's (reduce STN overactivity)

cofactor_dependencies.role: Cofactor for transaminases in Glu synthesis and for GAD (Glu → GABA)
NMDA receptor voltage-dependent block; glutamine synthetase cofactor

primary_regions: everywhere — glutamate is the brain's primary excitatory neurotransmitter

dsm_conditions.name: Epilepsy
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Excitatory/inhibitory imbalance favoring glutamate

dsm_conditions.name: Schizophrenia
dsm_conditions.mechanism: NMDA receptor hypofunction hypothesis (Olney & Farber 1995)

dsm_conditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Glutamate excitotoxicity (memantine targets this)

dsm_conditions.name: ALS
dsm_conditions.mechanism: EAAT2 loss → motor neuron excitotoxicity (riluzole reduces glutamate release)

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: HIGH — Glutamatergic activity underlies all EEG-recordable cortical potentials. ERPs, SSVEPs, motor imagery — all are glutamatergic oscillations. Direct electrical stimulation activates glutamatergic circuits.

neurotransmitters.name: Gamma-Aminobutyric Acid

synthesis_pathway.notes: GAD67 (cytoplasmic, constitutive) and GAD65 (synaptic vesicle-associated, activity-dependent). GAD67 synthesizes ~90% of brain GABA.

degradation.notes: GABA shunt: GABA → SSA → Succinate → Krebs cycle. Links inhibition to energy metabolism.

transporter.name: GABA Transporters
transporter.notes: GAT-1 is primary neuronal/glial transporter. Tiagabine (GAT-1 inhibitor) is an antiepileptic.

vesicular_transporter.name: Vesicular GABA Transporter
vesicular_transporter.notes: Also transports glycine — hence called Vesicular Inhibitory Amino Acid Transporter

receptors.ion_permeability: Cl- (inhibitory in adults; excitatory in neonates due to high intracellular Cl-)
receptors.subunit_composition: Most common: α1β2γ2 (60% of brain GABA-A receptors)

modulatory_sites.benzodiazepine: α/γ interface. Requires γ2 subunit. α1 = sedation, α2 = anxiolysis, α3 = muscle relaxation, α5 = memory
modulatory_sites.barbiturate: β subunit transmembrane domain. Prolongs Cl- channel opening
modulatory_sites.ethanol: Low-dose: δ-containing extrasynaptic receptors. High-dose: synaptic receptors

expression: ubiquitous — primary fast inhibition throughout brain
receptors.function: Fast inhibitory transmission (phasic: synaptic α1βγ2; tonic: extrasynaptic α4/6βδ)
receptors.clinical: Benzodiazepines (anxiety, seizures, insomnia), barbiturates, propofol (anesthesia), brexanolone (PPD — neurosteroid at δ-containing). GABRA1 mutations → epilepsy.

receptors.signaling: Decreases cAMP, activates GIRK K+ channels (postsynaptic), inhibits Ca2+ channels (presynaptic)
receptors.function: Slow inhibition, presynaptic inhibition of transmitter release, thalamic oscillation gating
receptors.clinical: Baclofen (agonist) for spasticity. GABA-B dysfunction in absence epilepsy (thalamic T-type Ca2+ channel interaction).

cofactor_dependencies.role: Obligatory cofactor for GAD (Glu → GABA) AND GABA-T (GABA degradation)
cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: CRITICAL — B6 deficiency causes seizures in neonates (pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy). Adults: anxiety, irritability, seizure susceptibility

primary_regions: everywhere — GABA is the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter (~20% of cortical neurons are GABAergic interneurons)

dsm_conditions.name: Epilepsy
dsm_conditions.mechanism: GABA deficit → excitatory/inhibitory imbalance

dsm_conditions.name: Generalized anxiety
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Reduced GABAergic tone in amygdala and cortex

dsm_conditions.name: Alcohol use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Ethanol potentiates GABA-A (tolerance → withdrawal seizures from GABA downregulation)

dsm_conditions.name: Insomnia
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Insufficient GABAergic sleep promotion

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: HIGH — GABAergic interneurons generate cortical oscillations (gamma band = PV+ interneurons, theta = SOM+ interneurons). All EEG rhythms depend on GABA-mediated synchronization. tDCS modulates GABA levels (measurable via MRS).

neurotransmitters.name: Glycine

synthesis_pathway.notes: Reversible reaction. Also produced from threonine and glyoxylate.

degradation.notes: Mitochondrial complex. Deficiency causes nonketotic hyperglycinemia (glycine encephalopathy).

transporter.name: Glycine Transporters
transporter.notes: GlyT1: astrocytic, regulates glycine at NMDA receptor glycine site. GlyT2: neuronal, recycles glycine at inhibitory synapses. GlyT1 inhibitors (sarcosine) investigated as NMDA enhancers for schizophrenia.

receptors.function: Fast inhibition in spinal cord and brainstem (analogous to GABA-A in brain)
receptors.clinical: Strychnine (antagonist) → convulsions. GLRA1 mutations → hyperekplexia (startle disease). Autoimmune anti-GlyR encephalitis.

receptors.role: Obligatory co-agonist at NMDA receptor GRIN1 subunit
receptors.notes: Glycine (or D-serine from astrocytes) must bind for NMDA channel to open. This is NOT the inhibitory glycine receptor.

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: LOW — Primarily spinal/brainstem. Relevant for spinal cord BCI interfaces.

neurotransmitters.name: Histamine

synthesis_pathway.notes: Histidine is an essential amino acid. All brain histamine neurons are in the tuberomammillary nucleus (TMN) of posterior hypothalamus.

degradation.notes: Primary CNS degradation route (not MAO). Then MAO-B converts tele-methylhistamine to tele-methylimidazoleacetic acid.

transporter.name: No specific reuptake transporter identified
transporter.notes: Histamine is primarily cleared by HNMT (enzymatic degradation), not reuptake. OCT3 (SLC22A3) may contribute to non-specific uptake.

receptors.function: Wakefulness, cortical arousal, allergic response
receptors.clinical: First-gen antihistamines (diphenhydramine) cause drowsiness by blocking brain H1. Second-gen (loratadine) don't cross BBB.

receptors.function: Gastric acid secretion, synaptic plasticity
receptors.clinical: Ranitidine/famotidine (H2 blockers) for GERD

receptors.function: Presynaptic autoreceptor (inhibits histamine release) AND heteroreceptor (inhibits other NT release: ACh, DA, 5-HT, NE, Glu)
receptors.clinical: Pitolisant (H3 inverse agonist) for narcolepsy — increases histamine release → wakefulness

receptors.function: Immune modulation, chemotaxis
receptors.clinical: Primarily peripheral immune function

cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Impaired histamine synthesis → drowsiness, altered circadian regulation

dsm_conditions.name: Narcolepsy
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Reduced histaminergic wakefulness drive (compounds orexin loss)

dsm_conditions.name: Insomnia
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Excessive histaminergic arousal (suvorexant works on orexin, not histamine)

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: LOW — TMN is deep hypothalamic. Histamine's effect on cortical arousal affects EEG state (wake vs sleep signatures).

neurotransmitters.name: Endorphins / Enkephalins / Dynorphins

synthesis_pathway.notes: POMC → different products depending on tissue: pituitary anterior lobe (ACTH + β-lipotropin), intermediate lobe (α-MSH + β-endorphin), hypothalamus (β-endorphin)

degradation.notes: Also: aminopeptidase N (ANPEP), dipeptidyl peptidase III

receptors.function: Analgesia, euphoria, reward, respiratory depression
receptors.clinical: Target of morphine, fentanyl, heroin. OPRM1 A118G polymorphism affects opioid sensitivity.

receptors.function: Analgesia (less potent than mu), anxiolysis, mood regulation
receptors.clinical: Less abuse potential than mu agonists. Investigated for depression.

receptors.function: Analgesia, dysphoria, stress response, anti-reward (opposes DA in NAcc)
receptors.clinical: KOR activation causes dysphoria (salvinorin A, pentazocine). KOR antagonists investigated for depression and addiction.

receptors.function: Anxiety modulation, pain modulation (complex — can be pro or anti-nociceptive), stress response
receptors.clinical: Investigational target for anxiety and pain

cofactor_dependencies.role: Opioid peptides are gene-encoded and processed by prohormone convertases
cofactor_dependencies.deficiency_effect: Not cofactor-dependent — regulated by gene expression and enzymatic processing

dsm_conditions.name: Opioid use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Exogenous opioids hijack mu receptor reward signaling

dsm_conditions.name: Major depression
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Endogenous opioid system dysregulation (kappa system hyperactivation)

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: LOW — Deep brainstem/subcortical. DBS of PAG used for intractable pain.

receptors.function: Pain transmission (especially sustained/burning pain), emesis, neuroinflammation, anxiety
receptors.clinical: Aprepitant (NK1 antagonist) for chemotherapy nausea. NK1 antagonists investigated for depression and PTSD.

neurotransmitters.name: Orexin / Hypocretin

synthesis_pathway.notes: Produced ONLY by ~70,000 neurons in the lateral hypothalamus. Loss of these neurons causes narcolepsy type 1.

receptors.function: Wakefulness, arousal, reward
Sleep/wake regulation (primary wakefulness maintainer)
receptors.clinical: Suvorexant, lemborexant (dual OX1R/OX2R antagonists) for insomnia

dsm_conditions.name: Narcolepsy type 1
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Autoimmune destruction of orexin neurons (>90% loss). CSF orexin-A <110 pg/mL is diagnostic.

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: LOW — Deep hypothalamic. But orexin system modulates cortical arousal detectable by EEG.

synthesis_pathway.notes: Synthesized in magnocellular neurons of PVN and SON of hypothalamus. Released from posterior pituitary (peripheral) AND from dendrites (central — volume transmission).

receptors.function: Social bonding, trust, maternal behavior, anxiolysis, uterine contraction
receptors.clinical: Intranasal oxytocin investigated for autism, social anxiety, PTSD. Pitocin for labor induction.

dsm_conditions.name: Autism spectrum disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Oxytocin system differences (research-based association, not causal claim)

synthesis_pathway.product: Arginine Vasopressin (9 amino acid cyclic peptide)
synthesis_pathway.notes: Synthesized in magnocellular (PVN, SON) and parvocellular (PVN) hypothalamic neurons. Released from posterior pituitary.

receptors.function: Vasoconstriction, aggression, pair bonding, social recognition
receptors.clinical: V1a promoter polymorphism linked to pair-bonding behavior (prairie vole studies)

receptors.function: ACTH release (HPA axis), stress response, social memory
receptors.clinical: V1b antagonists investigated for stress-related disorders

receptors.function: Water reabsorption (aquaporin-2 insertion)
receptors.clinical: Desmopressin (V2 agonist) for diabetes insipidus and enuresis

neurotransmitters.name: Nitric Oxide

synthesis_pathway.enzyme_gene: NOS1 (nNOS, neuronal) / NOS3 (eNOS, endothelial) / NOS2 (iNOS, inducible)
synthesis_pathway.notes: nNOS activated by Ca2+ influx through NMDA receptors. NO is not stored in vesicles — synthesized on demand and diffuses freely through membranes.

degradation.mechanism: Spontaneous oxidation to NO2- and NO3-
degradation.notes: Also reacts with superoxide (O2-) to form peroxynitrite (ONOO-) — toxic in excess

receptors.type: Soluble guanylyl cyclase (intracellular, NOT membrane receptor)
receptors.signaling: NO → activates sGC → GTP → cGMP → PKG activation
receptors.function: Vasodilation, synaptic plasticity (retrograde messenger at glutamatergic synapses), LTP modulation
receptors.notes: NO is a retrograde messenger — released from postsynaptic neuron, diffuses back to presynaptic terminal to enhance glutamate release

cofactor_dependencies.role: Essential NOS cofactor — NOS without BH4 produces superoxide instead of NO (uncoupled NOS)

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: MEDIUM — NO-mediated neurovascular coupling is the basis of fMRI BOLD signal and fNIRS. Disrupting NO affects BCI systems that rely on hemodynamic signals.

neurotransmitters.name: Endocannabinoids

synthesis_pathway.notes: Synthesized on demand from membrane lipids (not stored in vesicles). Released postsynaptically, acts as retrograde messenger.
2-AG is more abundant than AEA in brain. Primary retrograde messenger at most synapses.

degradation.notes: FAAH inhibitors (under investigation) prolong AEA signaling

receptors.signaling: Decreases cAMP, inhibits presynaptic Ca2+ channels, activates GIRK K+ channels
receptors.function: Retrograde inhibition of neurotransmitter release (both glutamate and GABA — called DSE and DSI respectively). Pain modulation, appetite, memory modulation.
receptors.clinical: THC (Δ9-THC) is CB1 partial agonist. Rimonabant (CB1 inverse agonist, withdrawn) caused depression/suicidality.
receptors.notes: CB1 is the most abundant GPCR in the brain

receptors.function: Immune modulation, neuroinflammation regulation
receptors.clinical: CB2 agonists investigated for neuroinflammation without psychoactive effects

dsm_conditions.name: Cannabis use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Exogenous CB1 activation → reward system hijacking and CB1 downregulation

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: LOW — Endocannabinoid signaling modulates synaptic plasticity but is not directly detectable by current BCI.

neurotransmitters.name: Adenosine / ATP (Purinergic)

synthesis_pathway.notes: ATP is co-released with classical neurotransmitters. Extracellular ATP is rapidly degraded to adenosine.
Intracellular adenosine production linked to methylation activity

receptors.function: Inhibition of neurotransmitter release, sleep promotion, neuroprotection
receptors.clinical: Caffeine is A1 (and A2A) antagonist → wakefulness by blocking adenosine sleep drive

receptors.function: Modulates DA D2 signaling (A2A-D2 heterodimer), motor function, wakefulness
receptors.clinical: Istradefylline (A2A antagonist) for Parkinson's (reduces off-time). Caffeine's motor-activating effect partly via striatal A2A.

receptors.function: Neuroinflammation, inflammasome activation, IL-1β release
receptors.clinical: P2X7 antagonists investigated for neuroinflammatory conditions, depression

primary_regions: ubiquitous — adenosine acts as neuromodulator throughout brain

dsm_conditions.name: Insomnia
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Adenosine accumulates during waking → promotes sleep via A1 and A2A in basal forebrain and VLPO

dsm_conditions.name: Caffeine use disorder
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Chronic A1/A2A antagonism → receptor upregulation → withdrawal symptoms

neurotransmitters.bci_relevance: LOW — Purinergic signaling is diffuse and not directly measured by current BCI.

receptors.function: Anxiolysis, appetite stimulation, vasoconstriction
Presynaptic inhibition, antiepileptic, appetite regulation
Appetite stimulation (orexigenic)

dsm_conditions.name: PTSD
dsm_conditions.mechanism: Low NPY levels associated with PTSD vulnerability (resilience biomarker)

dsm_conditions.name: Epilepsy
dsm_conditions.mechanism: NPY is endogenous anticonvulsant — Y2 receptor activation inhibits glutamate release

neurotransmitters.name: Epinephrine

synthesis_pathway.notes: PNMT expression in brain is very limited — mostly in C1-C3 neurons of medulla. Brain epinephrine is minor compared to peripheral (adrenal medulla).

neurotransmitters.precursor_pathway: Full chain: L-Phe → L-Tyr → L-DOPA → DA → NE → Epi (PNMT, SAM)

receptors.function: Vasoconstriction, arousal, PFC modulation at high NE
receptors.clinical: Prazosin (antagonist) for PTSD nightmares

receptors.signaling: Decreases cAMP, opens K+ channels, reduces Ca2+ entry
receptors.function: Presynaptic autoreceptor (reduces NE release); postsynaptic in PFC enhances working memory
receptors.clinical: Clonidine, guanfacine (α2A agonists) for ADHD, PTSD, hypertension

receptors.function: Heart rate increase, memory consolidation (amygdala-dependent)
receptors.clinical: Propranolol (antagonist) blocks emotional memory reconsolidation

receptors.function: Bronchodilation, glycogenolysis in astrocytes
receptors.clinical: Salbutamol (agonist) for asthma

neurotransmitters.notes: Minor neurotransmitter in brain. Primary role is peripheral (adrenal medulla → fight-or-flight). Included for completeness.

cofactor_master_list.name: Iron (Fe2+/Fe3+)
cofactor_master_list.transport: Transferrin (TF) in blood → Transferrin Receptor 1 (TFRC) at BBB → Ferroportin (SLC40A1) export. Brain iron stored in ferritin (FTH1, FTL). Ferritin requires iron for assembly.
excess_conditions: Neurodegeneration (iron accumulation in substantia nigra in Parkinson's, NBIA disorders)

cofactor_master_list.name: Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4)
cofactor_master_list.synthesis: GTP → GTP cyclohydrolase I (GCH1) → dihydroneopterin triphosphate → PTPS (PTS) → 6-pyruvoyltetrahydropterin → sepiapterin reductase (SPR) → BH4
cofactor_master_list.recycling: BH4 → BH2 (during hydroxylation) → BH4 via dihydropteridine reductase (QDPR) requiring NADH
cofactor_master_list.notes: BH4 is THE master cofactor for monoamine synthesis. Folate cycle supports BH4 regeneration.

cofactor_master_list.name: Vitamin B6 / Pyridoxal Phosphate (PLP)
cofactor_master_list.notes: PLP is cofactor for >140 enzymes. Critical for amino acid metabolism including neurotransmitter synthesis and degradation.

cofactor_master_list.name: Folate (Vitamin B9) / Tetrahydrofolate (THF)
cofactor_master_list.synthesis_of_active_form: Dietary folate → DHF → THF (via DHFR) → 5,10-MTHF → 5-MTHF (via MTHFR)
cofactor_master_list.notes: MTHFR C677T polymorphism reduces enzyme activity → impaired folate metabolism → elevated homocysteine. 5-MTHF donates methyl group to homocysteine → methionine (requires B12).

cofactor_master_list.name: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)
dependent_systems: SAM cycle (methionine synthase: homocysteine + 5-MTHF → methionine, gene: MTR, requires B12)
deficiency_conditions: Subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord
cofactor_master_list.notes: B12 deficiency impairs SAM cycle → affects ALL methylation reactions including COMT, DNA methylation, myelin synthesis. B12 is cobalt-containing.

cofactor_master_list.name: Copper (Cu2+)
cofactor_master_list.transport: Ceruloplasmin (CP) in blood. CTR1 (SLC31A1) for cellular uptake. ATP7A (Menkes), ATP7B (Wilson) for intracellular trafficking.
deficiency_conditions: Menkes disease (ATP7A mutation → copper deficiency → NE synthesis failure → neurodegeneration)
excess_conditions: Wilson disease (ATP7B mutation → copper accumulation → liver + brain damage, basal ganglia degeneration)

cofactor_master_list.name: Zinc (Zn2+)
cofactor_master_list.transport: ZnT (SLC30A) family exports zinc. ZIP (SLC39A) family imports zinc. ZnT3 (SLC30A3) loads zinc into synaptic vesicles at glutamatergic synapses.
cofactor_master_list.brain_role: Synaptically released zinc from glutamatergic vesicles modulates NMDA, AMPA, GABA-A receptors and glycine receptors. Particularly enriched in hippocampal mossy fibers.

cofactor_master_list.name: Magnesium (Mg2+)
cofactor_master_list.notes: Mg2+ blocks NMDA channel at resting potential. Depolarization relieves Mg2+ block → Ca2+ influx. This makes NMDA a coincidence detector.

cofactor_master_list.name: Calcium (Ca2+)
dependent_systems: ALL synaptic vesicle release (Ca2+-dependent exocytosis via synaptotagmin)
cofactor_master_list.notes: Intracellular Ca2+ is THE universal second messenger for neurotransmitter release. Maintained at ~100nM (10,000x lower than extracellular).

cofactor_master_list.name: Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)
cofactor_master_list.transport: SVCT2 (SLC23A2) transporter concentrates vitamin C in brain (10x plasma levels). Brain has highest concentration of any organ.

cofactor_master_list.name: Thiamine (Vitamin B1)
cofactor_master_list.notes: Thiamine deficiency selectively damages mammillary bodies, medial thalamus, periaqueductal gray. Common in alcoholism.

cofactor_master_list.name: S-Adenosylmethionine (SAM)
cofactor_master_list.regeneration: SAM → SAH → Homocysteine → Methionine (requires B12 + folate) → SAM. This is the methyl cycle.
cofactor_master_list.notes: SAM donates methyl groups for >100 methylation reactions. Its regeneration requires B12, folate, and methionine — linking nutrition to neurotransmitter metabolism.
